title: The fake citation landscape: examples from the wild - Research Information
content:
Nick Morley looks at real examples of hallucinated citations in published research, and explains what they mean for science

You’ve probably heard of academic publishing’s hottest new foe: fake references, or hallucinated citations. This demon comes in many forms and poses a major threat to quality and reputation. In this article we’ll explore a range of real examples that made it past peer review into published literature, to help you understand the risk and what you can do about it.

title: The localisation and accessibility of preprint services: implications for Open Science - MetaROR
content: Abstract:  Preprint services now play a key role in disseminating research across a wide range of domains. In this paper we examine where a set of 64 preprint services, collated by ASAPbio, are being physically hosted and the type of Internet Service Provider (ISP) they are using. In addition to this access to these services from 106 territories was simulated using Virtual Private Networks (VPNs). We find that the majority of services (47/64) are physically based in the USA, despite instances where the Top Level Domain (TLD) indicates a different country. In addition to this 56/64 of the services are being hosted by commercial ISPs with 43/64 being hosted by Cloudflare, Google LLC or Amazon. Poorer countries are more likely to encounter an error when attempting a URL corresponding to the landing page of one of the preprint services collated by ASAPbio than more wealthy countries. Sites that are physically located in Low or Middle Income Countries have similar accessibilities and may be better. We draw some overall conclusions about the potential frailty of these services with respect to the technical decisions made here.

title: From first-come, first-served to rubric-based Open Access Publishing Funds: A comparative analysis and survey study - ScienceDirect
content: Abstract: Open Access (OA) publishing contributes to research visibility and compliance with grant mandates, yet high Article Processing Charges remain a major barrier. To address this, Clemson University Libraries launched an Open Access Publishing Fund (OAPF) in 2016, initially using a first-come, first-served model. Rapid depletion of funds under this model prompted a shift in 2023 to a rubric-based monthly award system. This research compared the current practices of Clemson University's OAPF with OA funds at similar land-grant universities. The findings revealed that all eight institutions used first-come, first-served models and had similar eligibility criteria to Clemson's. It also surveyed previous OAPF applicants to assess how the OAPF can effectively support their OA publishing needs at Clemson University. Survey results indicated high priority for applicants without alternative funding, early-career faculty, and graduate students/post docs. Respondents also supported journal quality criteria, such as Directory of Open Access Journals listing and high impact factor. Based on the survey results, Clemson Libraries redesigned its OAPF rubric to reflect researchers'

title: Uneven observability in the interpretation of open access, altmetrics, and citations: evidence from Cuban social sciences | Research Square
content: Abstract: Research metrics are often interpreted as if they operated over a stable and fully observable universe of publications. This article examines how uneven observability reshapes comparisons across open access, altmetric attention, and citations in an underrepresented research setting. Using Cuban social sciences output from 2020 to 2024 as an analytical case, we constructed a cleaned OpenAlex corpus of 8,493 publications after an exploratory comparison of candidate databases for source selection. Altmetric data were retrieved through the Altmetric Details Page API for the subset with valid digital object identifiers (DOIs). The analysis distinguishes three observability layers: the full corpus, publications with DOIs, and publications with both DOIs and observable altmetric attention. Of the total corpus, 4,410 publications had DOIs and only 342 showed provider-retrievable attention. As the observable universe narrowed, its composition changed substantially in terms of open access, citation levels, and thematic profile. Detectable attention was concentrated mainly on X and Mendeley, open access pathways showed distinct observability and citation patterns, and the

title: As open as possible? Comparing Scandinavian approaches to research security | Science and Public Policy | Oxford Academic
content: Abstract:  Intensifying geopolitical rivalries have triggered a series of national and international policy initiatives designed to safeguard research security and reduce risks associated with international scientific collaboration. This paper undertakes a comparative analysis of Denmark, Norway, and Sweden, examining the structures, processes, and policy frames that shape their respective approaches to research security and responsible internationalization. Although the Scandinavian countries share cultural, linguistic, and political similarities, as well as advanced research capabilities, their institutionalized strategies for managing the tension between openness and security diverge in significant ways. By examining the policy frames and the interplay between structural and processual factors, the study reveals how these variations produce distinct governance outcomes. Through this analysis, the paper contributes to conceptualizing a typology of national approaches, offering insights into how small, open knowledge economies navigate the dual imperatives of international engagement and strategic protection in an era of geopolitical uncertainty.

title: DIGITAL INFRASTRUCTURE AND CAPACITY BUILDING ON RESEARCH DATA MANAGEMENT IN AFRICAN LIBRARY SETTINGS by UZOAGBA, Ngozi Celestina (PhD, CLN)
content: Abstract: This study investigates the impact of digital infrastructure and capacity building on Research Data Management (RDM) services in African libraries, emphasizing their critical roles in enhancing research productivity and data accessibility. Digital infrastructure including institutional repositories, reliable internet connectivity, cloud storage, and interoperable platforms is foundational for enabling effective RDM by facilitating data storage, preservation, and sharing in compliance with global standards such as the FAIR principles. However, infrastructural challenges such as limited broadband access, unstable power supply, and inadequate technological resources remain prevalent across many African countries, including Nigeria, conscapacity building libraries’ ability to provide sustainable RDM services. Complementing infrastructure, targeted capacity building and skill development for librarians are essential to develop competencies in data curation, metadata management, and policy adherence, which directly influence the quality and sustainability of RDM services. title: The Six Camps of Metascience
content: "Hypothesis: A lack of transparency enables bad practices and slows progress. The case for openness is both that transparency is good in itself and that science advances faster when it’s open by default. Theory of change: Increase transparency → increase accountability → faster, more reliable science. Policy relevance: This perspective shows up in transparency requirements in agency grants and in open access mandates, such as the 2022 White House Office of Science and Technology Policy memorandum requiring publications (and underlying data) to be available without an embargo and Plan S, a mandate for immediate-publication access from a coalition of European research funders. It has produced practical infrastructure that many researchers now use regularly, such as preprints, preregistration, and registered reports. A representative policy question: should agencies mandate open data, preregistration, and open access for federally funded research? Evidence and methods: Historically, their approach has been normative and values-based, with some empirical analysis. The movement’s strength comes from a combination of advocacy-driven policy reform, platform development, and community

title: Publishing from within: a view from the Netherlands University Presses – Open Access Books Network
content: "The NUPs didn’t emerge because publishing needed another “player” but because many people working in and around universities, like librarians, researchers and editors, have grown increasingly uncomfortable with how scholarly publishing currently operates. High prices, limited access, and systems that reward brand over substance are all part of that discomfort. This challenge is not only cultural but structural, as it touches on how prestige is produced through evaluation systems, funding mechanisms, and publishing infrastructures that continue to privilege established commercial actors. The NUPs are trying to do things differently, and their integration into the library helps them do so, as their starting point is not profit, but service, while maintaining quality standards. This means thinking about what authors (teachers and researchers) actually need; how to make publishing processes clearer and transparent; and how to ensure that publications reach readers without barriers. In doing so, they are not only offering an alternative publishing route but also implicitly challenging what counts as prestige in the first place, shifting the focus from brand recognition and exclusivity

title: Where can I publish a book open access without (necessarily) paying a fee? A list of Diamond open access options
content: "It is important to note that there is virtually no 100% Diamond open access press – always contact a press you are interested in early on to discuss this option. Please note also that even many fully Diamond OA presses would prefer to receive a BPC if one is available to you at your institution, to help them to focus their support on authors who are unaffiliated or who do not have access to institutional funds such as many early career researchers.... These models are often referred to as ‘Diamond’ open access, to differentiate it from open access models in which fees are paid by authors. While there have been some recent moves, such as by the Diamond Discovery Hub, to change the definition of Diamond to exclude some publisher types, this list does not make this distinction and includes publishers of all type and size. It has been collated from my own knowledge gained during my time on the Copim Project, working on the Opening the Future Diamond OA funding model, and relies on details from public-facing publisher and funding scheme websites. It has been partially supplemented by the addition of a small number of European Diamond OA publishers from the Diamond Open Access Book

title: From Gatekeepers to Gateways: How Acceleration and Platform Logic Reshape Life‐Science Journals - Colangelo - 2026 - Learned Publishing - Wiley Online Library
content: "Open access was initially framed as a corrective to exclusionary subscription models, promising universal accessibility and democratised knowledge circulation. In terms of readership, this promise has been partially realized: barriers to access have diminished for many institutions and independent scholars. Yet the prevailing APC-based implementation introduces a different axis of inequality (Knöchelmann 2021). When publication fees become the primary revenue engine, the capacity to disseminate research rapidly and visibly becomes tied to institutional purchasing power. Transformative agreements and central funding pools shield researchers at well-resourced universities from direct financial friction, whereas scholars in less affluent settings may confront delays, waiver negotiations, or strategic journal downgrading. Acceleration amplifies this asymmetry: in a regime where rapid publication enhances visibility and career mobility, those unable to finance participation risk systemic disadvantage. The marginalization at stake is therefore not merely individual but institutional, and often geopolitical. Diamond-OA initiatives and community-governed publishing models demonstrate that
snippet: "Open access was initially framed as a corrective to exclusionary subscription models, promising universal accessibility and democratised knowledge circulation. In terms of readership, this promise

title: Assessing institutional repositories for evidence of student learning: Journal of Electronic Resources Librarianship: Vol 0, No 0
content: Abstract:  Institutional repositories (IRs) are assessed via holdings and usage, yet institutions may require evidence of contributions to teaching, learning, and research status. Authors created two instruments: a multi-institutional survey of IR managers/administrators on assessment of IRs for student learning and IRs as factors in attaining R1 research status, and a single-institution survey on faculty perceptions of IRs for teaching/learning. Managers reported student-learning assessment is not a concern; faculty responses showed IRs are not widely viewed as teaching/learning tools. Findings suggest limited alignment between IR assessment and institutional goals. Librarians can improve IR promotion and develop rubrics/checklists to capture tacit outcomes.

title: F1000's VeriXiv introduces trust badges for preprints - Research Information
content:
"F1000 has introduced trust marker badges to its VeriXiv preprint server, providing readers with visible confirmation of the verification checks completed before research is posted online.

The open research publisher says the move makes VeriXiv the first major preprint platform to display comprehensive research integrity verification directly on published preprints.

Launched in August 2024 in collaboration with the Gates Foundation, VeriXiv was created to combine the speed of preprint dissemination with stronger quality assurance measures. Submissions are screened across four areas before publication: authorship verification, publishing ethics, research integrity and open research standards."

title: Open policy finder
content:
"Open policy finder is delighted to join TSOSI, an initiative that aggregates global data on financial support for open access infrastructures. Our missions align closely: both organisations are committed to the long-term sustainability of open science and to making visible the investments that enable equitable, global exchange of knowledge.

Our supporter information is now available through TSOSI at this link: TSOSI - Open Policy Finder. The dataset is still growing, and we'll continue to add and refine information over the coming months."

title: ORE Scientific Advisory Board members call
content: "As Open Research Europe (ORE) enters its new phase in autumn 2026, we are pleased to launch a call for nominations for membership of the new ORE Scientific Advisory Board (SAB). The aim is to establish the new SAB by September 2026. The SAB will play a key role in supporting the governance, strategic development, and integrity of ORE from the launch onwards. Its responsibilities include: Advising the Funders’ Group, the Executive Committee, and the Operating Organisation (CERN) on scientific matters related to the publishing services and its policies (e.g. author guidelines), as well as on ethical guidelines and the peer review process. Advising the Funders’ Group, Executive Committee, and Operating Organisation on strategic aspects of the platform, including its future direction, sustainability, potential new services, and opportunities for collaboration. Reviewing and providing advice on misconduct cases and transparency issues. Submitting an annual written report to the Funders’ Group on its activities. The report shall include an assessment of the publishing services offered by ORE to the research community and recommendations for improvement. The Funders’ Secretariat will

title: Leo Waaijers Open Science Award | NWO
content:
"In 2024, UKB (University Libraries & National Library) established the Leo Waaijers Open Science Prize. The prize was established in memory of Leo Waaijers (1938–2023), a tireless advocate of open access and free access to academic publications. The Leo Waaijers Open Science Prize aims to recognise outstanding contributions to open science in the Netherlands by putting the spotlight on teams or individuals who have demonstrated exceptional commitment to the principles of open science. By highlighting these achievements, the prize seeks to inspire others to apply and promote open science, stimulate innovation and strengthen the open science movement.

From 2026, NWO and UKB will join forces in a combined NWO UKB Leo Waaijers Open Science Prize. In addition to the honour, the prize consists of a statuette and a cash award of € 50,000, provided by NWO. With the Leo Waaijers Open Science Prize, NWO and UKB aim to inspire others to follow in his footsteps. The prize will be presented during the Open Science Festival, which Open Science NL organises annually in collaboration with partner organisations."

title: Using digital repositories to share impactful research – Cornell University Library
content: "When Cornell Ph.D. student Jena Shields needed an online repository to hold the data for a scientific paper she co-authored with her advisor and other peers on how dandelion seeds break off and scatter, she turned to Cornell University Library’s eCommons platform. Research data librarian Lencia McKee guided Shields through the process of uploading, error-testing, and formatting her data sets."

title: Understanding the Implementation of Open Research Training across UK Institutions
content: Abstract: This qualitative evaluation study explores the barriers and enablers of implementation of open research training at participants’ institutions following attendance at a UK Reproducibility Network (UKRN) Train-the-Trainer (T3) course, delivered within the UKRN Open Research Programme (September 2021—August 2026). It addresses three questions: • How do T3 participants implement, adapt, and embed open research training within their institutions? • What institutional conditions act as barriers or enablers of that embedding? • What individual, social, and structural factors shape whether these practices become normalised in institutional culture and practice? Small group interviews (n=9 participants across four sessions) were analysed using Normalisation Process Theory (NPT) as a deductive coding framework. The NPT constructs were operationalised to capture how trainers made sense of the programme, engaged with it, enacted it, and evaluated it. Trainers typically described themselves as motivated and capable, and recognised the value of training on Open Research. However, effective participation in the onward delivery expected as part of the Train-the-Trainer programme

title: New UKRN Working Paper explores how to embed open research training across institutions
content:
"UKRN has released a new working paper examining how open research training can be effectively implemented and sustained across UK higher education institutions.

Working Paper 11, Understanding the Implementation of Open Research Training across UK Institutions, draws on qualitative interviews with participants in the UKRN Open Research Programme’s Train-the-Trainer (T3) initiative. The study investigates how training is adapted and delivered locally, and what factors influence whether it becomes embedded in institutional practice and culture.

The findings highlight a consistent theme: while participants are highly motivated and recognise the value of open research, successful onward delivery depends less on the training itself and more on local institutional conditions. Time, recognition, coordination structures, and leadership support all play a critical role in determining whether training translates into sustained practice change."

title: Mapping the Landscape of Metadata Research in the Era of Open Science: A Comprehensive Bibliometric Analysis of Last Three Decades (1996–2025): Journal of Library Metadata: Vol 0, No 0
content: Abstract: Over the last thirty years, metadata has emerged as a strong component of digital scholarship, interoperability, and research data management. The paper is based on extensive bibliometric study of metadata research over a period of 30 years (1996–2025), using 403 publications that are included in the Web of Science. The study explores publication growth, intellectual structure, influential sources, and patterns of global collaboration using descriptive indicators, performance metrics, and science mapping techniques, such as coauthorship, key word co-occurrence, Bradfords Law, and three field plots using Bibliometrix R and VOSviewer. Findings indicate that the growth rate remained constant at 8.45%, which indicates the persistent scholarly interest and maturity of metadata as a research field. A wide and growing thematic range is indicated by the high citation average (109.3 citations per document) and the size of author keywords. The kernel of research output and scholarly impact is made up of a limited number of core journals: Library Resources and Technical Services, Electronic Library, Journal of Documentation and Scientific Data. The visualization of key words in the

title: Open, but Uncounted: Citations to Open Access Preprints and Working Papers of Published Papers
content: Abstract: The citations to open access preprint and working paper versions of papers that are also formally published in journals are not regularly counted in global multidisciplinary proprietary citation index databases, nor are they considered in most bibliometric studies. They are, however, integrated into paper citation counts in other citation databases. It can be argued that open access preprint citations reflect a relevant part of scientific impact of a work as proxied by citation counts, particularly in scientific disciplines that make extensive use of preprints/working papers for rapid open communication. Here we present a large-scale study of these usually uncounted open access preprint citations for the Web of Science database to provide an exploratory overview of the magnitude of this phenomenon and its distribution across disciplines. To this end, we introduce a methodology to link cited reference strings referring to preprints/working papers to their matching published journal versions. We find that at least 390,000 WoS-indexed publications have 1.1 million preprint version citations which are not included in ordinary citation counts.
snippet: Abstract: The citations to open access preprint and working paper versions of papers that are also formally published in journals are not regularly counted in global multidisciplinary proprietary

title: Knowledge Futures and Crossref: Exploring High-Volume DOI Management
content:
"For many years, PubPub has made it possible for communities to assign DOIs to a range of outputs and component Pubs. Knowledge Futures and Crossref are building together to test the limits of what’s possible for high-volume, high-granularity DOI management. That means fast prototypes, real building, and learning through the process....

This is also an example of where Knowledge Futures is headed more generally. We’re taking what we’ve learned from building publishing infrastructure and applying it across different parts of the scholarly communication ecosystem. Not siloed within PubPub development, but open to building more broadly and collaborating across organizational lines."

title: Celebrating 10 years of the DOAJ Ambassador Programme – DOAJ Blog
content:
"Ten years ago, in 2016, we launched the DOAJ Ambassador Programme with 15 Ambassadors who were recruited to improve open access publishing in the Global South. Today, we have 14 Ambassadors, representing 9 regions and operating from 12 countries, some of whom are also DOAJ team members, and some who have been  Ambassadors since the very beginning! One of the programme’s greatest successes has been its ability to connect journals and people across regions, languages and publishing contexts. 

The DOAJ Ambassador programme was made possible due to a grant from the International Development Research Centre (IDRC), and time dedicated by DOAJ team members to build the foundations of the Ambassador programme. After the grant concluded in 2017, the Ambassador programme took a new shape as an integrated part of DOAJ instead of a project supported by a grant. Some Ambassadors became volunteers or team members, and the programme developed organically until 2024, when the Ambassador programme was reviewed and redefined with new processes and foundations."

title: Pivoting for access: How OSU Libraries is leading the next chapter of e-book strategy | Oklahoma State University
content: "The OSU Libraries has steadily shifted focus from aggregator platforms, choosing instead to work directly with publishers who meet the Library’s preference for unlimited-user, digital rights management (DRM) access. This approach not only saves money but changes experiences for OSU students and faculty. The OSU Libraries works with a wide range of publishers that support these access models, ensuring patrons benefit from flexible, sustainable e-book options."

title: Van Loon | Faculty Perspectives on Open Access: A Multidimensional Analysis of Open-Access Usage and Library Relationships at an R2 Institution | Journal of Librarianship and Scholarly Communication
content: Abstract: Introduction: The focus of this study is the broad impact open-access (OA) publishing has on all aspects of faculty members’ work (including publishing habits, research activities, and teaching) and on faculty relationships with the library. Methods: An online survey addressing the impact of OA publishing on faculty and on their relationship with the library was administered to full-time tenure-track faculty at an R2 institution. Responses were analyzed by disciplinary unit, and themes were developed from faculty qualitative responses. Results: A majority of faculty respondents had experience with OA publishing, with payment of article processing charges (APCs) to OA publishers, and with the use of OA materials in research and teaching. Many faculty respondents reported a substantial impact of OA on their research, teaching, and authorship, but expressed concern with the funding of OA publishing fees, with the quality and reputation of OA journals, and with the ethics of the OA business model. Discussion: Faculty respondents demonstrate differing levels of familiarity with OA materials in all three roles. Overall, they are more receptive to OA materials as readers and

title: LBU Research Voices – What open research means to me | Leeds Beckett University
content: "In our latest LBU Research Voices interview, we met up with Margot Avella, a PhD student in Criminology and Psychology. Margot talks about her research into understanding the behaviour of serial rapists, and how she has taken her first steps into open research practices to ensure her research is transparent and available to other researchers working in her field."

title: Why the Gates Foundation Abandoned Article Processing Charges (and What They’re Doing Instead) | Katina Magazine
content: "In 2024, the Gates Foundation updated their Open Access Policy to redirect funds from APCs to support more equitable models. Ashley Farley explains their thinking and vision for the future."

title: Open Source 2.0 : From Open Source Software to Open Source Resources?
content: Abstract:  What is « open source »? Commonly associated with software whose source code is available under a licence following the « Open Source Definition » (OSD) of the Open Source Initiative (OSI), the definition of open source remains a major source of confusion and conflict. Within software communities the OSD is controversial, and with the democratisation of other openness movements (open science, open education, open hardware, etc.) there is an increasing question regarding open source beyond software for digital resources where source files are provided. The topic of « open source resources » can be essential to enable modifiability of openly available resources in these various domains, greatly enhancing the ability to collaborate. Based on the review of more than 200 documents related to debates and usages of the notion, conducted as independent citizen research, this paper argues for a paradigm shift in the very meaning of « open source ».
